entries.title: In NetChoice Cases, Supreme Court Labels a Surprisingly Narrow Class of Online Platform Company Activities as Protected Expression
entries.subtitle: Analysis of the Supreme Court's landmark decision on First Amendment protections for social media platform content curation practices.
entries.abstract: This article examines the Supreme Court's combined decision in Moody v. NetChoice and NetChoice v. Paxton, which addressed the scope of First Amendment protections for online platform companies' content curation activities. The Court adopted a narrow interpretation, recognizing protected expression primarily in enforcement of platforms' content and community guidelines while signaling skepticism toward broader protections for algorithm-driven or non-content-based curatorial practices. The decision provides guidance that platform curation activities must be evaluated granularly rather than categorically, leaving substantial room for legislative regulation of platform design and content-agnostic practices.

entries.title: Section 230 and Child Exploitation: Restoring the Original Intent of the Communications Decency Act
entries.subtitle: A critical analysis of how Section 230 has been distorted from its original child protection purpose to provide near-absolute immunity to tech platforms.
entries.publisher: Mary Graw Leary / Villanova Law Review
entries.abstract: This article examines Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act, arguing that the statute was originally intended to protect children from exploitation online rather than to provide broad platform immunity. Through legislative history analysis and review of contemporaneous media coverage from 1996, the author demonstrates a significant gap between Section 230's original purposes and current internet realities, particularly regarding Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM). The article proposes reforms to restore Section 230 to its original intent while addressing contemporary digital harms.

entries.title: Section 230 After NetChoice: What the Court Left Unsaid
entries.subtitle: A close reading of the 2024 NetChoice opinions, the doctrinal questions they resolved, and the larger questions they deliberately avoided.
entries.abstract: The Supreme Court's 2024 NetChoice opinions were widely anticipated as a landmark statement on platform speech. This analysis examines what the Court actually held Ã¢ÂÂ as opposed to what it was asked to decide Ã¢ÂÂ and maps the doctrinal questions left open for lower courts, Congress, and the next wave of platform litigation.

entries.title: Compelled Speech and Algorithmic Curation After Moody v. NetChoice
entries.subtitle: Examines whether must-carry and must-not-carry obligations on platforms constitute compelled speech under the First Amendment as clarified by Moody.
entries.abstract: State statutes requiring platforms to carry certain content Ã¢ÂÂ or prohibiting them from removing it Ã¢ÂÂ raise compelled speech questions that the Court's NetChoice opinions addressed only obliquely. This analysis applies the Court's reasoning to the must-carry/must-not-carry distinction and assesses the constitutional viability of content moderation mandates.

entries.title: FTC v. Amazon: What the Complaint Reveals About Modern Monopolization Theory
entries.subtitle: An analysis of the FTC's Amazon complaint as a window into how enforcement agencies are attempting to modernize Sherman Act Section 2 doctrine.
entries.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington
entries.abstract: The FTC's 2023 complaint against Amazon marks an ambitious attempt to apply Section 2 of the Sherman Act to a platform ecosystem rather than a discrete market. This analysis reads the complaint as a doctrinal argument Ã¢ÂÂ examining the market definition choices, the conduct theories, and the remedial logic Ã¢ÂÂ and assesses their prospects under existing case law.

entries.title: Loper Bright and the Future of FCC Rulemaking Authority
entries.subtitle: Traces the implications of the Court's Chevron overruling for the FCC's ability to promulgate broadband and platform regulations.
entries.abstract: The Supreme Court's overruling of Chevron deference in Loper Bright has immediate consequences for agencies that have long relied on their own statutory interpretations to justify expansive rulemaking. This analysis focuses on the FCC, mapping the regulations most vulnerable to post-Loper Bright challenge and considering what statutory revision would be required to restore the agency's authority.

entries.title: Copyright and the Training Data Question: Where Courts Are Headed
entries.subtitle: Surveys pending litigation over AI training data and sketches the likely doctrinal paths courts will take to resolve the fair use question.
entries.abstract: A wave of copyright litigation targeting AI training practices is working its way through the federal courts. This analysis surveys the pending cases, maps the competing fair use arguments, and sketches the doctrinal paths most likely to produce a stable resolution Ã¢ÂÂ including the possibility that Congress will need to act regardless of how courts rule.
